/commodity/price/spot.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.commodity.price.spot()
obb.commodity.price.spot(commodity='wti')
```

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

fred.name: commodity
fred.description: Commodity name associated with the EIA spot price commodity data, default is 'all'.

fred.name: frequency
fred.description:
Frequency aggregation to convert high frequency data to lower frequency.
        None = No change
        a = Annual
        q = Quarterly
        m = Monthly
        w = Weekly
        d = Daily
        wef = Weekly, Ending Friday
        weth = Weekly, Ending Thursday
        wew = Weekly, Ending Wednesday
        wetu = Weekly, Ending Tuesday
        wem = Weekly, Ending Monday
        wesu = Weekly, Ending Sunday
        wesa = Weekly, Ending Saturday
        bwew = Biweekly, Ending Wednesday
        bwem = Biweekly, Ending Monday

fred.name: aggregation_method
fred.description:
A key that indicates the aggregation method used for frequency aggregation.
        This parameter has no affect if the frequency parameter is not set.
        avg = Average
        sum = Sum
        eop = End of Period

fred.name: transform
fred.description:
Transformation type
        None = No transformation
        chg = Change
        ch1 = Change from Year Ago
        pch = Percent Change
        pc1 = Percent Change from Year Ago
        pca = Compounded Annual Rate of Change
        cch = Continuously Compounded Rate of Change
        cca = Continuously Compounded Annual Rate of Change
        log = Natural Log

standard.description: Commodity name.

standard.description: Price of the commodity.

standard.description: Unit of the commodity price.

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get the EIA's Weekly Petroleum Status Report.

obb.commodity.petroleum_status_report(category='weekly_estimates', table='imports')
```

/commodity/short_term_energy_outlook.description:
Monthly short term (18 month) projections using EIA's STEO model.

Source: www.eia.gov/steo/
/commodity/short_term_energy_outlook.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get the EIA's Short Term Energy Outlook.
obb.commodity.short_term_energy_outlook()
# Select the specific table of data from the STEO. Table 03d is World Crude Oil Production.
obb.commodity.short_term_energy_outlook(table='03d')
```

standard.name: start_date

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

eia.name: symbol
eia.description: Symbol to get data for. If provided, overrides the 'table' parameter to return only the specified symbol from the STEO API.

eia.name: table
eia.description:
The specific table within the STEO dataset. Default is '01'. When 'symbol' is provided, this parameter is ignored.

    03e: World Petroleum and Other Liquid Fuels Consumption
    04a: US Petroleum and Other Liquid Fuels Supply, Consumption, and Inventories
    04b: US Hydrocarbon Gas Liquids (HGL) and Petroleum Refinery Balances
    04c: US Regional Motor Gasoline Prices and Inventories
    04d: US Biofuel Supply, Consumption, and Inventories
    05a: US Natural Gas Supply, Consumption, and Inventories
    05b: US Regional Natural Gas Prices
    06: US Coal Supply, Consumption, and Inventories
    07a: US Electricity Industry Overview
    07b: US Regional Electricity Retail Sales
    07c: US Regional Electricity Prices
    07d1: US Regional Electricity Generation, Electric Power Sector
    07d2: US Regional Electricity Generation, Electric Power Sector, continued
    07e: US Electricity Generating Capacity
    08: US Renewable Energy Consumption
    09a: US Macroeconomic Indicators and CO2 Emissions
    09b: US Regional Macroeconomic Data
    09c: US Regional Weather Data
    10a: Drilling Productivity Metrics
    10b: Crude Oil and Natural Gas Production from Shale and Tight Formations

/commodity/psd_data.description: Get data tables and historical time series from the USDA FAS Production, Supply, and Distribution (PSD) Reports.
/commodity/psd_data.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get the World Crop Production Summary table.
obb.commodity.psd_data()
# Get the current Corn World Trade table from the PDS report.
obb.commodity.psd_data(report_id='corn_world_trade')
# Get all attributes for Coffee globally, for a single year.
obb.commodity.psd_data(commodity='coffee', start_year=2025, end_year=2025)
# Compare Brazil coffee exports versus the world from 2010 to present.
obb.commodity.psd_data(commodity='coffee', country='brazil', attribute='exports', aggregate_regions=True, start_year=2010)
# Get historical production of corn in the US from 2020.
obb.commodity.psd_data(commodity='corn', country='united_states', attribute='production', start_year=2020)
# Get regional aggregates for wheat beginning and ending stocks from 2020.
obb.commodity.psd_data(commodity='wheat', country='world', attribute='beginning_stocks,ending_stocks', aggregate_regions=True, start_year=2020)
```

/commodity/psd_report.description:
Agriculture commodity production, supply, and distribution PDF reports (World Agricultural Outlook).

This command returns only the results portion of the OBBject response.
It contains a dictionary where the PDF content is base64 encoded under the 'content' key.
/commodity/psd_report.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.commodity.psd_report(commodity='sugar', year=2022, month=5)
# Get the PSD report for coffee for March 2023.
obb.commodity.psd_report(commodity='coffee', year=2023, month=3)
```

standard.name: commodity
standard.description: Commodity for the report.

x-widget_config.description: Commodity for the report to retrieve.

standard.name: year
standard.description: Year of the report.

x-widget_config.description: Year of the report to retrieve. Data is available from 2006 onwards.

standard.name: month
standard.description: Month of the report.

x-widget_config.description: Month of the report to retrieve.

standard.name: content
standard.description: Base64 encoded content.

widget_config.name: USDA FAS Commodity Production Supply & Distribution Reports
widget_config.description: Monthly publications released by the USDA Foreign Agriculture Service.

/commodity/weather_bulletins.description:
Get current and historical weather bulletins with their PDF links.

This command returns only the results portion of the OBBject response.
It contains a list of dictionaries where each dictionary has 'label' and 'value' keys.

Use this endpoint to programmatically access the list of available weather bulletins.
Suitable for dropdown selections in a UI.
/commodity/weather_bulletins.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get weather bulletins for the current year.
obb.commodity.weather_bulletins()
# Get weather bulletins for May 2023, week 2.
obb.commodity.weather_bulletins(year=2023, month=5, week=2)
# Get URLs for comparing versus 1 year ago and download the base64-encoded PDF content to memory.
from datetime import datetime
urls = []
for year in [datetime.now().year, datetime.now().year - 1]:
    urls.append(obb.commodity.weather_bulletins(year=year, month=5, week=2)[0]['value'])
pdfs = obb.commodity.weather_bulletins_download(urls=urls)
# PDFs are now in a list where each item has 'content' and 'data_format' keys
```

standard.name: year
standard.description: Year of the data. Default is the current year.

standard.name: month
standard.description: Month of the data. If not provided, data for the entire year is returned.

standard.name: week
standard.description: Numeric week of the data, relative to the month. If not provided, data for the entire month is returned.

standard.name: label
standard.description: Label representing the weather bulletin file.

standard.name: value
standard.description: URL to the weather bulletin document.

/commodity/weather_bulletins_download.description:
Download one, or more, weather bulletin documents.

This command returns only the results portion of the OBBject response.
It contains a list of dictionaries where the base64 encoded content of the document is under the 'content' key.

standard.name: urls
standard.type: str | dict | list | list[str | dict | list]
standard.description: URLs for reports to download. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): government_us.

standard.name: content
standard.description: Base64 encoded content of the weather bulletin document.

widget_config.name: USDA Weather & Crop Bulletin
widget_config.description: Weekly Weather and Crop Bulletin from the USDA.

/crypto/price/historical.description: Get historical price data for cryptocurrency pair(s) within a provider.
/crypto/price/historical.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.crypto.price.historical(symbol='BTCUSD')
obb.crypto.price.historical(symbol='BTCUSD', start_date='2024-01-01', end_date='2024-01-31')
# Get monthly historical prices from Yahoo Finance for Ethereum.
obb.crypto.price.historical(symbol='ETH-USD', interval='1m', start_date='2024-01-01', end_date='2024-12-31')
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): fmp, tiingo, yfinance.

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date

/currency/price/historical.description:
Currency Historical Price. Currency historical data.

Currency historical prices refer to the past exchange rates of one currency against
another over a specific period.
This data provides insight into the fluctuations and trends in the foreign exchange market,
helping analysts, traders, and economists understand currency performance,
evaluate economic health, and make predictions about future movements.
/currency/price/historical.examples:

```python

obb.currency.price.historical(symbol='EURUSD')

```

/currency/search.description:
Currency Search.

Search available currency pairs.
Currency pairs are the national currencies from two countries coupled for trading on
the foreign exchange (FX) marketplace.
Both currencies will have exchange rates on which the trade will have its position basis.
All trading within the forex market, whether selling, buying, or trading, will take place through currency pairs.
(ref: Investopedia)
Major currency pairs include pairs such as EUR/USD, USD/JPY, GBP/USD, etc.
/currency/search.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.currency.search()
# Search for 'EUR' currency pair using 'intrinio' as provider.
obb.currency.search(query='EUR')
```

/currency/reference_rates.description:
Get current, official, currency reference rates.

Foreign exchange reference rates are the exchange rates set by a major financial institution or regulatory body,
serving as a benchmark for the value of currencies around the world.
These rates are used as a standard to facilitate international trade and financial transactions,
ensuring consistency and reliability in currency conversion.
They are typically updated on a daily basis and reflect the market conditions at a specific time.
Central banks and financial institutions often use these rates to guide their own exchange rates,
impacting global trade, loans, and investments.

/derivatives/options/surface.description:
Filter and process the options chains data for volatility.

Data posted can be an instance of OptionsChainsData,
a pandas DataFrame, or a list of dictionaries.
Data should contain the fields:

- `expiration`: The expiration date of the option.
- `strike`: The strike price of the option.
- `option_type`: The type of the option (call or put).
- `implied_volatility`: The implied volatility of the option. Or 'target' field.
- `open_interest`: The open interest of the option.
- `volume`: The trading volume of the option.
- `dte` : Optional, days to expiration (DTE) of the option.
- `underlying_price`: Optional, the price of the underlying asset.

Results from the `/derivatives/options/chains` endpoint are the preferred input.

If `underlying_price` is not supplied in the data as a field, it must be provided as a parameter.
/derivatives/options/surface.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Filter and process options chains data for volatility.
data = obb.derivatives.options.chains('AAPL', provider='cboe')
surface = obb.derivatives.options.surface(data=data.results, moneyness=20, dte_min=10, dte_max=60, chart=True)
surface.show()
```

standard.name: data
standard.type: Data | ForwardRef('Data') | ForwardRef('DataFrame') | ForwardRef('Series') | ForwardRef('ndarray') | dict | list

standard.name: target
standard.description: The field to use as the z-axis. Default is "implied_volatility".

standard.name: underlying_price
standard.description: The price of the underlying asset.

standard.name: option_type
standard.description: The type of df to display. Default is "otm". Choices are: ["otm", "itm", "puts", "calls"]

standard.name: dte_min
standard.description: Minimum days to expiration (DTE) to filter options.

standard.name: dte_max
standard.description: Maximum days to expiration (DTE) to filter options.

standard.name: moneyness
standard.description: Specify a % moneyness to target for display, entered as a value between 0 and 100.

standard.name: strike_min
standard.description: Minimum strike price to filter options.

standard.name: strike_max
standard.description: Maximum strike price to filter options.

standard.name: oi
standard.description: Filter for only options that have open interest. Default is False.

standard.name: volume
standard.description: Filter for only options that have trading volume. Default is False.

standard.name: theme
standard.description: The theme to use for the chart. Default is "dark". Only valid if `openbb-charting` is installed.

standard.name: chart_params
standard.description: Additional parameters to pass to the charting library. Only valid if `openbb-charting` is installed. Valid keys are: - `title`: The title of the chart. - `xtitle`: Title for the x-axis. - `ytitle`: Title for the y-axis. - `ztitle`: Title for the z-axis. - `colorscale`: The colorscale to use for the chart. - `layout_kwargs`: Additional dictionary to be passed to `fig.update_layout` before output.

standard.name: chart
standard.description: Whether to create a chart or not, by default False.

/econometrics/correlation_matrix.description:
Get the correlation matrix of an input dataset.

The correlation matrix provides a view of how different variables in your dataset relate to one another.
By quantifying the degree to which variables move in relation to each other, this matrix can help identify patterns,
trends, and potential areas for deeper analysis. The correlation score ranges from -1 to 1, with -1 indicating a
perfect negative correlation, 0 indicating no correlation, and 1 indicating a perfect positive correlation.
/econometrics/correlation_matrix.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get the correlation matrix of a dataset.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.correlation_matrix(data=stock_data)
obb.econometrics.correlation_matrix(data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

standard.name: method
standard.description: Method to use for correlation calculation. Default is "pearson". pearson : standard correlation coefficient kendall : Kendall Tau correlation coefficient spearman : Spearman rank correlation

standard.description: Whether to create a chart or not, by default False.

/econometrics/ols_regression.description:
Perform Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression.

OLS regression is a fundamental statistical method to explore and model the relationship between a
dependent variable and one or more independent variables. By fitting the best possible linear equation to the data,
it helps uncover how changes in the independent variables are associated with changes in the dependent variable.
This returns the model and results objects from statsmodels library.
/econometrics/ols_regression.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.ols_regression(data=stock_data, y_column="close", x_columns=["open", "high", "low"])
obb.econometrics.ols_regression(y_column='close', x_columns='['open', 'high', 'low']', data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/econometrics/autocorrelation.description:
Perform Durbin-Watson test for autocorrelation.

The Durbin-Watson test is a widely used method for detecting the presence of autocorrelation in the residuals
from a statistical or econometric model. Autocorrelation occurs when past values in the data series influence
future values, which can be a critical issue in time-series analysis, affecting the reliability of
model predictions. The test provides a statistic that ranges from 0 to 4, where a value around 2 suggests
no autocorrelation, values towards 0 indicate positive autocorrelation, and values towards 4 suggest
negative autocorrelation. Understanding the degree of autocorrelation helps in refining models to better capture
the underlying dynamics of the data, ensuring more accurate and trustworthy results.
/econometrics/autocorrelation.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform Durbin-Watson test for autocorrelation.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.autocorrelation(data=stock_data, y_column="close", x_columns=["open", "high", "low"])
obb.econometrics.autocorrelation(y_column='close', x_columns='['open', 'high', 'low']', data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/econometrics/residual_autocorrelation.description:
Perform Breusch-Godfrey Lagrange Multiplier tests for residual autocorrelation.

The Breusch-Godfrey Lagrange Multiplier test is a sophisticated tool for uncovering autocorrelation within the
residuals of a regression model. Autocorrelation in residuals can indicate that a model fails to capture some
aspect of the underlying data structure, possibly leading to biased or inefficient estimates.
By specifying the number of lags, you can control the depth of the test to check for autocorrelation,
allowing for a tailored analysis that matches the specific characteristics of your data.
This test is particularly valuable in econometrics and time-series analysis, where understanding the independence
of errors is crucial for model validity.
/econometrics/residual_autocorrelation.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform Breusch-Godfrey Lagrange Multiplier tests for residual autocorrelation.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.residual_autocorrelation(data=stock_data, y_column="close", x_columns=["open", "high", "low"])
obb.econometrics.residual_autocorrelation(y_column='close', x_columns='['open', 'high', 'low']', data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/econometrics/cointegration.description:
Show co-integration between two timeseries using the two step Engle-Granger test.

The two-step Engle-Granger test is a method designed to detect co-integration between two time series.
Co-integration is a statistical property indicating that two or more time series move together over the long term,
even if they are individually non-stationary. This concept is crucial in economics and finance, where identifying
pairs or groups of assets that share a common stochastic trend can inform long-term investment strategies
and risk management practices. The Engle-Granger test first checks for a stable, long-term relationship by
regressing one time series on the other and then tests the residuals for stationarity.
If the residuals are found to be stationary, it suggests that despite any short-term deviations,
the series are bound by an equilibrium relationship over time.
/econometrics/cointegration.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform co-integration test between two timeseries.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.cointegration(data=stock_data, columns=["open", "close"])
```

standard.description: Data columns to check cointegration

/econometrics/causality.description:
Perform Granger causality test to determine if X 'causes' y.

The Granger causality test is a statistical hypothesis test to determine if one time series is useful in
forecasting another. While 'causality' in this context does not imply a cause-and-effect relationship in
the philosophical sense, it does test whether changes in one variable are systematically followed by changes
in another variable, suggesting a predictive relationship. By specifying a lag, you set the number of periods to
look back in the time series to assess this relationship. This test is particularly useful in economic and
financial data analysis, where understanding the lead-lag relationship between indicators can inform investment
decisions and policy making.
/econometrics/causality.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform Granger causality test to determine if X 'causes' y.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.causality(data=stock_data, y_column="close", x_column="open")
# Example with mock data.
obb.econometrics.causality(y_column='close', x_column='open', lag=1, data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/econometrics/unit_root.description:
Perform Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) unit root test.

The ADF test is a popular method for testing the presence of a unit root in a time series.
A unit root indicates that the series may be non-stationary, meaning its statistical properties such as mean,
variance, and autocorrelation can change over time. The presence of a unit root suggests that the time series might
be influenced by a random walk process, making it unpredictable and challenging for modeling and forecasting.
The 'regression' parameter allows you to specify the model used in the test: 'c' for a constant term,
'ct' for a constant and trend term, and 'ctt' for a constant, linear, and quadratic trend.
This flexibility helps tailor the test to the specific characteristics of your data, providing a more accurate
assessment of its stationarity.
/econometrics/unit_root.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Perform Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) unit root test.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='fmp').to_df()
obb.econometrics.unit_root(data=stock_data, column="close")
obb.econometrics.unit_root(data=stock_data, column="close", regression="ct")
obb.econometrics.unit_root(column='close', data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/econometrics/panel_random_effects.description:
Perform One-way Random Effects model for panel data.

One-way Random Effects model to panel data is offering a nuanced approach to analyzing data that spans across both
time and entities (such as individuals, companies, countries, etc.). By acknowledging and modeling the random
variation that exists within these entities, this method provides insights into the general patterns that
emerge across the dataset.
/econometrics/panel_random_effects.examples:

--------

/econometrics/panel_between.description:
Perform a Between estimator regression on panel data.

The Between estimator for regression analysis on panel data is focusing on the differences between entities
(such as individuals, companies, or countries) over time. By aggregating the data for each entity and analyzing the
average outcomes, this method provides insights into the overall impact of explanatory variables (x_columns) on
the dependent variable (y_column) across all entities.
/econometrics/panel_between.examples:
Examples
--------

/econometrics/panel_pooled.description:
Perform a Pooled coefficient estimator regression on panel data.

The Pooled coefficient estimator for regression analysis on panel data is treating the data as a large
cross-section without distinguishing between variations across time or entities
(such as individuals, companies, or countries). By assuming that the explanatory variables (x_columns) have a
uniform effect on the dependent variable (y_column) across all entities and time periods, this method simplifies
the analysis and provides a generalized view of the relationships within the data.
/econometrics/panel_pooled.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.econometrics.panel_pooled(y_column='portfolio_value', x_columns='['risk_free_rate']', data='[{'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_1', 'time': 0, 'portfolio_value': 100000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.02}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_1', 'time': 1, 'portfolio_value': 150000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_2', 'time': 0, 'portfolio_value': 150000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_2', 'time': 1, 'portfolio_value': 133333.33, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_3', 'time': 0, 'portfolio_value': 133333.33, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_3', 'time': 1, 'portfolio_value': 125000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_4', 'time': 0, 'portfolio_value': 125000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.03}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_4', 'time': 1, 'portfolio_value': 120000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.02}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_5', 'time': 0, 'portfolio_value': 120000.0, 'risk_free_rate': 0.02}, {'is_multiindex': True, 'multiindex_names': "['asset_manager', 'time']", 'asset_manager': 'asset_manager_5', 'time': 1, 'portfolio_value': 116666.67, 'risk_free_rate': 0.02}]')
```

/econometrics/panel_fixed.description:
One- and two-way fixed effects estimator for panel data.

The Fixed Effects estimator to panel data is enabling a focused analysis on the unique characteristics of entities
(such as individuals, companies, or countries) and/or time periods. By controlling for entity-specific and/or
time-specific influences, this method isolates the effect of explanatory variables (x_columns) on the dependent
variable (y_column), under the assumption that these entity or time effects capture unobserved heterogeneity.
/econometrics/panel_fixed.examples:
Examples
--------

/econometrics/panel_first_difference.description:
Perform a first-difference estimate for panel data.

The First-Difference estimator for panel data analysis is focusing on the changes between consecutive observations
for each entity (such as individuals, companies, or countries). By differencing the data, this method effectively
removes entity-specific effects that are constant over time, allowing for the examination of the impact of changes
in explanatory variables (x_columns) on the change in the dependent variable (y_column).
/econometrics/panel_first_difference.examples:
Examples
--------

/econometrics/panel_fmac.description:
Fama-MacBeth estimator for panel data.

The Fama-MacBeth estimator, a two-step procedure renowned for its application in finance to estimate the risk
premiums and evaluate the capital asset pricing model. By first estimating cross-sectional regressions for each
time period and then averaging the regression coefficients over time, this method provides insights into the
relationship between the dependent variable (y_column) and explanatory variables (x_columns) across different
entities (such as individuals, companies, or countries).
/econometrics/panel_fmac.examples:
Examples
--------

/econometrics/variance_inflation_factor.description:
Calculate VIF (variance inflation factor), which tests for collinearity.

It quantifies the severity of multicollinearity in an ordinary least squares regression analysis. The square
root of the variance inflation factor indicates how much larger the standard error increases compared to if
that variable had 0 correlation to other predictor variables in the model.

It is defined as:

$ VIF_i = 1 / (1 - R_i^2) $
where $ R_i $ is the coefficient of determination of the regression equation with the column i being the result
from the i:th series being the exogenous variable.

A VIF over 5 indicates a high collinearity and correlation. Values over 10 indicates causes problems, while a
value of 1 indicates no correlation. Thus VIF values between 1 and 5 are most commonly considered acceptable.
In order to improve the results one can often remove a column with high VIF.

For further information see: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variance_inflation_factor
/econometrics/variance_inflation_factor.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Calculate the variance inflation factor.
stock_data = obb.equity.price.historical(symbol='TSLA', start_date='2023-01-01', provider='yfinance').to_df()
obb.econometrics.variance_inflation_factor(data=stock_data, columns=["open", "high", "low", "close"])
obb.econometrics.variance_inflation_factor(columns='['open', 'high', 'low']', data='[{'date': '2023-01-02', 'open': 110.0, 'high': 120.0, 'low': 100.0, 'close': 115.0, 'volume': 10000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-03', 'open': 165.0, 'high': 180.0, 'low': 150.0, 'close': 172.5, 'volume': 15000.0}, {'date': '2023-01-04', 'open': 146.67, 'high': 160.0, 'low': 133.33, 'close': 153.33, 'volume': 13333.33}, {'date': '2023-01-05', 'open': 137.5, 'high': 150.0, 'low': 125.0, 'close': 143.75, 'volume': 12500.0}, {'date': '2023-01-06', 'open': 132.0, 'high': 144.0, 'low': 120.0, 'close': 138.0, 'volume': 12000.0}]')
```

/economy/survey/bls_search.description: Search BLS surveys by category and keyword or phrase to identify BLS series IDs.
/economy/survey/bls_search.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.survey.bls_search(category='cpi')
# Use semi-colon to separate multiple queries as an & operator.
obb.economy.survey.bls_search(category='cpi', query='seattle;gasoline')
```

standard.name: query
standard.description: The search word(s). Use semi-colon to separate multiple queries as an & operator.

bls.name: category
bls.description:
The category of BLS survey to search within.
        An empty search query will return all series within the category. Options are:
        
    cpi - Consumer Price Index
        
    pce - Personal Consumption Expenditure
        
    ppi - Producer Price Index
        
    ip - Industry Productivity
        
    jolts - Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey
        
    nfp - Nonfarm Payrolls
        
    cps - Current Population Survey
        
    lfs - Labor Force Statistics
        
    wages - Wages
        
    ec - Employer Costs
        
    sla - State and Local Area Employment
        
    bed - Business Employment Dynamics
        
    tu - Time Use

bls.name: include_extras
bls.description: Include additional information in the search results. Extra fields returned are metadata and vary by survey. Fields are undefined strings that typically have names ending with '_code'.

bls.name: include_code_map
bls.description: When True, includes the complete code map for eaçh survey in the category, returned separately as a nested JSON to the `extras['results_metadata']` property of the response. Example content is the NAICS industry map for PPI surveys. Each code is a value within the 'symbol' of the time series.

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol representing the entity requested in the data.

standard.name: title
standard.description: The title of the series.

standard.name: survey_name
standard.description: The name of the survey.

/economy/survey/manufacturing_outlook_ny.description:
Get the Empire State Manufacturing Survey.

It is a monthly survey of manufacturers in New York State conducted by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

Participants from across the state in a variety of industries respond to a questionnaire
and report the change in a variety of indicators from the previous month.

Respondents also state the likely direction of these same indicators six months ahead.
April 2002 is the first report, although survey data date back to July 2001.

The survey is sent on the first day of each month to the same pool of about 200
manufacturing executives in New York State, typically the president or CEO.

About 100 responses are received. Most are completed by the tenth, although surveys are accepted until the fifteenth.
/economy/survey/manufacturing_outlook_ny.examples:

```python
from openbb import obb

```

/economy/cpi.description: Get Consumer Price Index (CPI) data by country.
/economy/cpi.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.cpi(country='japan,china,turkey')
# Use the `transform` parameter to define the reference period for the change in values. Default is YoY.
obb.economy.cpi(country='united_states,united_kingdom', transform='period')
# Get the latest reported weightings of a country's CPI basket, from IMF.
res = obb.economy.cpi(provider='imf', country='CAN', transform='weight_percent', expenditure='all', limit=1)
print(res.model_dump(include='results')['results'])
```

/economy/balance_of_payments.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.balance_of_payments()
obb.economy.balance_of_payments(country='brazil')
obb.economy.balance_of_payments()
obb.economy.balance_of_payments(report_type='summary')
obb.economy.balance_of_payments()
obb.economy.balance_of_payments(country='japan')
# The `country` parameter will override the `report_type`.
obb.economy.balance_of_payments(country='united_states')
```

/economy/fred_search.description:
Search for FRED series or economic releases by ID or string.

This does not return the observation values, only the metadata.
Use this function to find series IDs for `fred_series()`.
/economy/fred_search.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.fred_search()
```

standard.name: query
standard.description: The search word(s).

fred.name: search_type
fred.description: The type of search to perform. Automatically set to 'release' when a 'release_id' is provided.

fred.name: release_id
fred.description: A specific release ID to target.

fred.name: limit
fred.description: The number of data entries to return. (1-1000)

fred.name: offset
fred.description: Offset the results in conjunction with limit. This parameter is ignored When search_type is 'release'.

fred.name: order_by
fred.description: Order the results by a specific attribute. The default is 'observation_end'.

fred.name: sort_order
fred.description: Sort the 'order_by' item in ascending or descending order. The default is 'desc'.

fred.name: filter_variable
fred.description: Filter by an attribute.

fred.name: filter_value
fred.description: String value to filter the variable by.  Used in conjunction with filter_variable. This parameter is ignored when search_type is 'release'.

fred.name: tag_names
fred.description: A semicolon delimited list of tag names that series match all of.  Example: 'japan;imports' This parameter is ignored when search_type is 'release'.

fred.name: exclude_tag_names
fred.description: A semicolon delimited list of tag names that series match none of.  Example: 'imports;services'. Requires that variable tag_names also be set to limit the number of matching series. This parameter is ignored when search_type is 'release'.

fred.name: series_id
fred.description: A FRED Series ID to return series group information for. This returns the required information to query for regional data. Not all series that are in FRED have geographical data. Entering a value for series_id will override all other parameters. Multiple series_ids can be separated by commas.

standard.name: release_id
standard.description: The release ID for queries.

standard.name: series_id
standard.description: The series ID for the item in the release.

standard.name: series_group
standard.description: The series group ID of the series. This value is used to query for regional data.

standard.name: region_type
standard.description: The region type of the series.

standard.name: name
standard.description: The name of the release.

standard.name: title
standard.description: The title of the series.

standard.name: observation_start
standard.description: The date of the first observation in the series.

standard.name: observation_end
standard.description: The date of the last observation in the series.

standard.name: frequency
standard.description: The frequency of the data.

standard.name: frequency_short
standard.description: Short form of the data frequency.

standard.name: units
standard.description: The units of the data.

standard.name: units_short
standard.description: Short form of the data units.

standard.name: seasonal_adjustment
standard.description: The seasonal adjustment of the data.

standard.name: seasonal_adjustment_short
standard.description: Short form of the data seasonal adjustment.

standard.name: last_updated
standard.description: The datetime of the last update to the data.

standard.name: popularity
standard.description: Popularity of the series

standard.name: group_popularity
standard.description: Group popularity of the release

standard.name: realtime_start
standard.description: The realtime start date of the series.

standard.name: realtime_end
standard.description: The realtime end date of the series.

standard.name: notes
standard.description: Description of the release.

standard.name: press_release
standard.description: If the release is a press release.

standard.name: url
standard.description: URL to the release.

/economy/fred_series.description: Get data by series ID from FRED.
/economy/fred_series.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.fred_series(symbol='NFCI')
# Multiple series can be passed in as a list.
obb.economy.fred_series(symbol='NFCI,STLFSI4')
# Use the `transform` parameter to transform the data as change, log, or percent change.
obb.economy.fred_series(symbol='CBBTCUSD', transform='pc1')
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): fred.

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: limit
standard.description: The number of data entries to return.

fred.name: limit
fred.description: The number of data entries to return.

fred.name: frequency
fred.description:
Frequency aggregation to convert high frequency data to lower frequency.
    None = No change
    a = Annual
    q = Quarterly
    m = Monthly
    w = Weekly
    d = Daily
    wef = Weekly, Ending Friday
    weth = Weekly, Ending Thursday
    wew = Weekly, Ending Wednesday
    wetu = Weekly, Ending Tuesday
    wem = Weekly, Ending Monday
    wesu = Weekly, Ending Sunday
    wesa = Weekly, Ending Saturday
    bwew = Biweekly, Ending Wednesday
    bwem = Biweekly, Ending Monday

fred.name: aggregation_method
fred.description:
A key that indicates the aggregation method used for frequency aggregation.
        This parameter has no affect if the frequency parameter is not set.
        avg = Average
        sum = Sum
        eop = End of Period

fred.name: transform
fred.description:
Transformation type
    None = No transformation
    chg = Change
    ch1 = Change from Year Ago
    pch = Percent Change
    pc1 = Percent Change from Year Ago
    pca = Compounded Annual Rate of Change
    cch = Continuously Compounded Rate of Change
    cca = Continuously Compounded Annual Rate of Change
    log = Natural Log

intrinio.name: all_pages
intrinio.description: Returns all pages of data from the API call at once.

intrinio.name: sleep
intrinio.description: Time to sleep between requests to avoid rate limiting.

standard.name: date
standard.description: The date of the data.

intrinio.name: value
intrinio.description: Value of the index.

/economy/fred_release_table.description: Get economic release data by ID and/or element from FRED.
/economy/fred_release_table.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# Get the top-level elements of a release by not supplying an element ID.
obb.economy.fred_release_table(release_id='50')
# Drill down on a specific section of the release.
obb.economy.fred_release_table(release_id='50', element_id='4880')
# Drill down on a specific table of the release.
obb.economy.fred_release_table(release_id='50', element_id='4881')
```

standard.name: release_id
standard.description: The ID of the release. Use `fred_search` to find releases.

standard.name: element_id
standard.description: The element ID of a specific table in the release.

standard.name: date
standard.type: None | date | str | None | list[None | date | str | None]
standard.description: A specific date to get data for. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): fred.

standard.name: date
standard.description: The date of the data.

standard.name: level
standard.description: The indentation level of the element.

standard.name: element_type
standard.description: The type of the element.

standard.name: line
standard.description: The line number of the element.

standard.name: element_id
standard.description: The element id in the parent/child relationship.

standard.name: parent_id
standard.description: The parent id in the parent/child relationship.

standard.name: children
standard.description: The element_id of each child, as a comma-separated string.

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol representing the entity requested in the data.

standard.name: name
standard.description: The name of the series.

standard.name: value
standard.description: The reported value of the series.

standard.name: m2

/economy/unemployment.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.unemployment()
obb.economy.unemployment(country='all', frequency='quarter')
# Demographics for the statistics are selected with the `age` parameter.
obb.economy.unemployment(country='all', frequency='quarter', age='total')
```

standard.name: country
standard.description: The country to get data. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): oecd.

standard.name: frequency
standard.description: The frequency of the data.

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

oecd.name: country
oecd.description: The country to get data.

oecd.name: sex
oecd.description: Sex to get unemployment for.

oecd.name: age
oecd.description: Age group to get unemployment for. Total indicates 15 years or over

oecd.name: seasonal_adjustment
oecd.description: Whether to get seasonally adjusted unemployment. Defaults to False.

standard.name: date
standard.description: The date of the data.

standard.name: country
standard.description: Country for which unemployment rate is given

standard.name: value
standard.description: Unemployment rate, as a normalized percent.

/economy/composite_leading_indicator.description:
Get the composite leading indicator (CLI).

It is designed to provide early signals of turning points
in business cycles showing fluctuation of the economic activity around its long term potential level.

CLIs show short-term economic movements in qualitative rather than quantitative terms.
/economy/composite_leading_indicator.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.composite_leading_indicator()
obb.economy.composite_leading_indicator(country='all', growth_rate=True)
```

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

oecd.name: country
oecd.description: Country to get the CLI for, default is G20.

oecd.name: adjustment
oecd.description: Adjustment of the data, either 'amplitude' or 'normalized'. Default is amplitude.

oecd.name: growth_rate
oecd.description: Return the 1-year growth rate (%) of the CLI, default is False.

standard.name: date
standard.description: The date of the data.

standard.name: value
standard.description: CLI value

standard.name: country
standard.description: Country for the CLI value.

/economy/fred_regional.description:
Query the Geo Fred API for regional economic data by series group.

The series group ID is found by using `fred_search` and the `series_id` parameter.
/economy/fred_regional.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.fred_regional(symbol='NYICLAIMS')
# With a date, time series data is returned.
obb.economy.fred_regional(symbol='NYICLAIMS', start_date='2021-01-01', end_date='2021-12-31', limit=10)
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for.

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: limit
standard.description: The number of data entries to return.

fred.name: symbol
fred.description: For this function, it is the series_group ID or series ID. If the symbol provided is for a series_group, set the `is_series_group` parameter to True. Not all series that are in FRED have geographical data.

fred.name: is_series_group
fred.description: When True, the symbol provided is for a series_group, else it is for a series ID.

fred.name: region_type
fred.description: The type of regional data. Parameter is only valid when `is_series_group` is True.

fred.name: season
fred.description: The seasonal adjustments to the data. Parameter is only valid when `is_series_group` is True.

fred.name: units
fred.description: The units of the data. This should match the units returned from searching by series ID. An incorrect field will not necessarily return an error. Parameter is only valid when `is_series_group` is True.

fred.name: frequency
fred.description:
Frequency aggregation to convert high frequency data to lower frequency.
        
    None = No change
        
    a = Annual
        
    q = Quarterly
        
    m = Monthly
        
    w = Weekly
        
    d = Daily
        
    wef = Weekly, Ending Friday
        
    weth = Weekly, Ending Thursday
        
    wew = Weekly, Ending Wednesday
        
    wetu = Weekly, Ending Tuesday
        
    wem = Weekly, Ending Monday
        
    wesu = Weekly, Ending Sunday
        
    wesa = Weekly, Ending Saturday
        
    bwew = Biweekly, Ending Wednesday
        
    bwem = Biweekly, Ending Monday

fred.name: aggregation_method
fred.description:
A key that indicates the aggregation method used for frequency aggregation.
        This parameter has no affect if the frequency parameter is not set.
        
    avg = Average
        
    sum = Sum
        
    eop = End of Period

fred.name: transform
fred.description:
Transformation type
        
    None = No transformation
        
    chg = Change
        
    ch1 = Change from Year Ago
        
    pch = Percent Change
        
    pc1 = Percent Change from Year Ago
        
    pca = Compounded Annual Rate of Change
        
    cch = Continuously Compounded Rate of Change
        
    cca = Continuously Compounded Annual Rate of Change
        
    log = Natural Log

fred.description: The observation value. The units are defined in the search results by series ID.

fred.name: series_id
fred.description: The individual series ID for the region.

econdb.name: use_cache

imf.name: query

/economy/indicators.description: Get economic indicators by country and indicator.
/economy/indicators.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.indicators(symbol='PCOCO')
# Enter the country as the full name, or iso code. Use `/economy/available_indicators` to get a list of supported indicators from EconDB.
obb.economy.indicators(symbol='CPI', country='united_states,jp')
# Use the `main` symbol to get the group of main indicators for a country.
obb.economy.indicators(symbol='main', country='eu')
# IMF indicators are identified by their dataflow and indicator code. Use `/economy/available_indicators` to get and search a list of supported indicators symbols. This example gets gold reserves held by countries, measured in Fine Troy Ounces.
obb.economy.indicators(symbol='IL::RGV_REVS', country='*', frequency='month', limit=1, start_date='2025-09-30')
# IMF symbols can also be used for retrieving entire presentation tables. This example gets the Direct Investment Position (DIP) table. Use `/imf_utils/list_tables` to get a list of supported presentation table symbols.
obb.economy.indicators(symbol='DIP::H_DIP_INDICATOR', country='BRA', frequency='annual', limit=2, pivot=True)
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): econdb, imf, oecd.

x-widget_config.label: Symbol
x-widget_config.description: OECD indicator symbol in 'DATAFLOW::INDICATOR' format.

standard.name: country
standard.description: The country to get data. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): econdb, imf, oecd.

x-widget_config.label: Country
x-widget_config.description: Country or region for the table.

x-widget_config.label: Country
x-widget_config.description: Country or reference area.

standard.name: frequency
standard.description: The frequency of the data.

x-widget_config.label: Frequency
x-widget_config.description: The data frequency.

x-widget_config.label: Frequency
x-widget_config.description: The observation frequency.

standard.name: start_date
standard.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

standard.name: end_date
standard.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

econdb.name: symbol
econdb.description: Symbol to get data for. The base symbol for the indicator (e.g. GDP, CPI, etc.). Use `available_indicators()` to get a list of available symbols.

econdb.name: country
econdb.description: The country to get data. ISO country codes or country names.

econdb.name: frequency
econdb.description: The frequency of the data, default is 'quarter'. Only valid when 'symbol' is 'main'.

econdb.name: transform
econdb.description:
The transformation to apply to the data, default is None.

    tpop: Change from previous period
    toya: Change from one year ago
    tusd: Values as US dollars
    tpgp: Values as a percent of GDP

    Only 'tpop' and 'toya' are applicable to all indicators. Applying transformations across multiple indicators/countries may produce unexpected results.
    This is because not all indicators are compatible with all transformations, and the original units and scale differ between entities.
    `tusd` should only be used where values are currencies.

econdb.name: use_cache
econdb.description: If True, the request will be cached for one day. Using cache is recommended to avoid needlessly requesting the same data.

imf.name: symbol
imf.description:
Symbol to get data for. Symbol format: 'dataflow::identifier' where identifier is either:
- A table ID (starts with 'H_') for hierarchical table data
- An indicator code for individual indicator data

Examples:
    - 'BOP::H_BOP_BOP_AGG_STANDARD_PRESENTATION' - Balance of Payments table
    - 'BOP_AGG::GS_CD,BOP_AGG::GS_DB' - Multiple BOP_AGG indicators (Goods & Services)
    - 'IL::RGV_REVS' - Gold reserves in millions of fine troy ounces
    - 'WEO::NGDP_RPCH' - Real GDP growth (annual only)
    - 'WEO::POILBRE' - Brent crude oil price (use country='G001' for world)
    - 'PCPS::PGOLD' - Gold price per troy ounce (monthly/quarterly available)

Use `obb.economy.available_indicators(provider='imf')` to discover symbols. Use `obb.economy.imf_utils.list_tables()` to see available tables.

imf.name: country
imf.description: ISO3 country code(s). Use comma-separated values for multiple countries. Validated against the dataflow's available countries via constraint API.

imf.name: frequency
imf.description: The frequency of the data. Choices vary by indicator and country. Common options: 'annual', 'quarter', 'month'. Use 'all' or '*' to return all available frequencies. Direct IMF codes (e.g., 'A', 'Q', 'M') are also accepted.

imf.name: transform
imf.description: Transformation to apply to the data. User-friendly options: 'index' (raw values), 'yoy' (year-over-year %), 'period' (period-over-period %). Use 'all' or '*' to return all available transformations. Direct IMF codes (e.g., 'USD', 'IX') are also accepted.

x-widget_config.label: Transform
x-widget_config.description: Transformation to apply to the data.

imf.name: dimension_values
imf.description: list of additional dimension filters in 'DIM_ID:DIM_VALUE' format. Parameter can be entered multiple times.

x-widget_config.label: Dimension Filters
x-widget_config.description: Additional dimension filters in 'DIM_ID:DIM_VALUE' format. See IMF Dataflow Parameters widget for all possible combinations.

imf.name: limit
imf.description: Maximum number of records to retrieve per series.

x-widget_config.label: Limit
x-widget_config.description: Most recent N records to retrieve per series.

imf.name: pivot
imf.description: If True, pivots the data to presentation view with 'indicator' and 'country' as the index, date as values.

oecd.name: symbol
oecd.description: OECD indicator symbol(s). Format: 'DATAFLOW::INDICATOR' or 'DATAFLOW::TABLE_ID'. Multiple symbols from the same dataflow can be comma-separated.

oecd.name: country
oecd.description: Country name or ISO code. Comma-separated for multiples. 'all' for all.

oecd.name: frequency
oecd.description: Frequency: 'annual'/'yearly', 'quarterly'/'quarter', 'monthly'/'month', or SDMX code (A/Q/M).

oecd.name: transform
oecd.description: Transformation code (dataflow-specific, e.g. 'GY' for growth rate).

oecd.name: dimension_values

x-widget_config.label: Dimension Filters

/economy/central_bank_holdings.description: Get the balance sheet holdings of a central bank.
/economy/central_bank_holdings.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
# The default is the latest Treasury securities held by the Federal Reserve.
obb.economy.central_bank_holdings()
# Get historical summaries of the Fed's holdings.
obb.economy.central_bank_holdings(summary=True)
# Get the balance sheet holdings as-of a historical date.
obb.economy.central_bank_holdings(date='2019-05-21')
# Use the `holding_type` parameter to select Agency securities, or specific categories or Treasury securities.
obb.economy.central_bank_holdings(holding_type='agency_debts')
```

federal_reserve.description: Type of holdings to return.

federal_reserve.name: summary
federal_reserve.description: If True, returns historical weekly summary by holding type. This parameter takes priority over other parameters.

federal_reserve.name: wam
federal_reserve.description: If True, returns weighted average maturity aggregated by agency or treasury securities. This parameter takes priority over `holding_type`, `cusip`, and `monthly`.

federal_reserve.name: monthly
federal_reserve.description: If True, returns historical data for all Treasury securities at a monthly interval. This parameter takes priority over other parameters, except `wam`. Only valid when `holding_type` is set to: 'all_treasury', 'bills', 'notesbonds', 'frn', 'tips'.

/economy/interest_rates.description:
Get interest rates by country(s) and duration.
Most OECD countries publish short-term, a long-term, and immediate rates monthly.
/economy/interest_rates.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.interest_rates()

# Multiple countries can be passed in as a list.
obb.economy.interest_rates(duration='long', country='united_kingdom,germany', frequency='monthly')
```

/economy/primary_dealer_fails.description:
Primary Dealer Statistics for Fails to Deliver and Fails to Receive.

Data from the NY Federal Reserve are updated on Thursdays at approximately
4:15 p.m. with the previous week's statistics.

For research on the topic, see:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/econres/notes/feds-notes/the-systemic-nature-of-settlement-fails-20170703.html

"Large and protracted settlement fails are believed to undermine the liquidity
and well-functioning of securities markets.

Near-100 percent pass-through of fails suggests a high degree of collateral
re-hypothecation together with the inability or unwillingness to borrow or buy the needed securities."
/economy/primary_dealer_fails.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.primary_dealer_fails()
# Transform the data to be percentage totals by asset class
obb.economy.primary_dealer_fails(unit='percent')
```

/economy/direction_of_trade.description:
Get Direction Of Trade Statistics from the IMF database.

The Direction of Trade Statistics (DOTS) presents the value of merchandise exports and
imports disaggregated according to a country's primary trading partners.
Area and world aggregates are included in the display of trade flows between major areas of the world.
Reported data is supplemented by estimates whenever such data is not available or current.
Imports are reported on a cost, insurance and freight (CIF) basis
and exports are reported on a free on board (FOB) basis.
Time series data includes estimates derived from reports of partner countries
for non-reporting and slow-reporting countries.
/economy/direction_of_trade.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.direction_of_trade(country='all', counterpart='china')
# Select multiple countries or counterparts by entering a comma-separated list. The direction of trade can be 'exports', 'imports', 'balance', or 'all'.
obb.economy.direction_of_trade(country='us', counterpart='world,eu', frequency='annual', direction='exports')
```

/economy/total_factor_productivity.description:
Total Factor Productivity (TFP)

A real-time, quarterly series on total factor productivity (TFP) for the U.S. business sector,
adjusted for variations in factor utilization - labor effort and capital's workweek.

The utilization adjustments follows Basu, Fernald, and Kimball (BFK, 2006).
Using relative prices and input-output information, the series is also decomposed into separate TFP
and utilization-adjusted TFP series for equipment investment (including consumer durables) and "consumption"
(defined as business output less equipment and consumer durables).

Labor includes an adjustment for "quality" or composition.
Capital services are also adjusted for changes in composition over time
(e.g. computers, other equipment, structures, and inventories).

Source: https://www.frbsf.org/research-and-insights/data-and-indicators/total-factor-productivity-tfp/
/economy/total_factor_productivity.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.economy.total_factor_productivity()
# Get summary data instead of the default quarterly time series.
obb.economy.total_factor_productivity(frequency='summary')
```

federal_reserve.name: frequency
federal_reserve.description: Type of data to return. 'quarter' for quarterly time series, 'annual' for annual time series, 'summary' for summary statistics (period means).

federal_reserve.name: start_date
federal_reserve.description: Start date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format. Only applicable for time series data (quarter/annual).

federal_reserve.name: end_date
federal_reserve.description: End date of the data, in YYYY-MM-DD format. Only applicable for time series data (quarter/annual).

federal_reserve.name: date
federal_reserve.description: The date of the data.

federal_reserve.name: d_y_prod
federal_reserve.description: Business output, expenditure (product) side. From NIPA tables, Gross Value Added: Total Business: Quantity Index.

federal_reserve.name: d_y_inc
federal_reserve.description: Business output, measured from income side. Nominal business output is GDI less nominal non-business output. Real business income uses expenditure-side deflator.

federal_reserve.name: d_y
federal_reserve.description: Output. Average of d_y_prod and d_y_inc (weighted equally). If d_y_inc not yet available, equals d_y_prod.

federal_reserve.name: d_hours
federal_reserve.description: Hours worked in the business sector. From BLS productivity and cost release.

federal_reserve.name: d_lp
federal_reserve.description: Business-sector labor productivity, defined as d_y - d_hours. Note: Labor productivity in the BLS productivity-and-cost release equals d_y_prod - d_hours.

federal_reserve.name: d_k
federal_reserve.description: Capital input. Perpetual inventory stocks calculated from disaggregated quarterly NIPA investment data, then growth rates are weighted by estimated rental prices.

federal_reserve.name: d_lq_bls_interpolated
federal_reserve.description: Labor composition/quality from BLS. Pre-1979 is interpolated annual BLS MFP estimate of labor composition (interpolated using Denton (1971) relative to changes in hours).

federal_reserve.name: d_lq_aaronson_sullivan
federal_reserve.description: Labor composition/quality following Aaronson-Sullivan. 1979:Q1 - present follows Aaronson and Sullivan (2001), as extended by Bart Hobijn and Joyce Kwok (FRBSF).

federal_reserve.name: d_lq
federal_reserve.description: Labor composition/quality actually used. Pre-1979 is d_lq_bls_interpolated, 1979:Q1 onward uses d_lq_aaronson_sullivan.

federal_reserve.name: alpha
federal_reserve.description: Capital's share of income (ratio between 0 and 1). Based primarily on NIPA data for the corporate sector, assuming private noncorporate factor shares match corporate shares.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp
federal_reserve.description: Business sector Total Factor Productivity. Calculated as d_y - alpha*d_k - (1-alpha)*(d_hours+d_lq), i.e., output growth less the contribution of capital and labor.

federal_reserve.name: d_util
federal_reserve.description: Utilization adjustment for capital and labor. Uses Basu, Fernald, Fisher, and Kimball (2013) estimates applied to quarterly data.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp_util
federal_reserve.description: Utilization-adjusted Total Factor Productivity. Calculated as d_tfp - d_util, adjusting for variations in factor utilization.

federal_reserve.name: relative_price
federal_reserve.description: Relative price growth of 'consumption' to price of 'equipment'. Measures the relative price of non-equipment goods and services to price of equipment (with consumer durables classified as equipment).

federal_reserve.name: inv_share
federal_reserve.description: Equipment and consumer durables share of business output (ratio between 0 and 1). Represents the proportion of output devoted to equipment investment and consumer durables.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp_i
federal_reserve.description: TFP in equipment and consumer durables sector. Calculated from d_tfp assuming that relative price growth reflects relative TFP growth.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp_c
federal_reserve.description: TFP in non-equipment business output ('consumption' goods and services). Calculated from d_tfp assuming that relative price growth reflects relative TFP growth.

federal_reserve.name: d_u_invest
federal_reserve.description: Utilization adjustment in producing investment goods. Uses estimates from Basu, Fernald, Fisher, and Kimball to calculate utilization for producing equipment and consumer durables.

federal_reserve.name: d_u_consumption
federal_reserve.description: Utilization adjustment in producing non-investment business output ('consumption'). Uses estimates from Basu, Fernald, Fisher, and Kimball to calculate utilization for producing non-investment goods and services.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp_i_util
federal_reserve.description: Utilization-adjusted TFP in producing equipment and consumer durables. Calculated as d_tfp_i - d_u_invest.

federal_reserve.name: d_tfp_c_util
federal_reserve.description: Utilization-adjusted TFP in producing non-equipment business output ('consumption'). Calculated as d_tfp_c - d_u_consumption.

federal_reserve.name: variable
federal_reserve.description: The variable name (e.g., 'd_y', 'd_tfp', 'd_tfp_util').

federal_reserve.name: variable_title
federal_reserve.description: Human-readable title for the variable.

federal_reserve.name: full_sample_mean
federal_reserve.description: Mean value over the full sample period.

federal_reserve.name: past_4_quarters
federal_reserve.description: Mean value over the past 4 quarters.

federal_reserve.name: past_8_quarters
federal_reserve.description: Mean value over the past 8 quarters.

federal_reserve.name: since_2019
federal_reserve.description: Mean value since 2019:Q4.

federal_reserve.name: period_2004_2019
federal_reserve.description: Mean value for the period 2004:Q4 to 2019:Q4.

federal_reserve.name: period_1995_2004
federal_reserve.description: Mean value for the period 1995:Q4 to 2004:Q4.

federal_reserve.name: period_1973_1995
federal_reserve.description: Mean value for the period 1973:Q1 to 1995:Q4.

federal_reserve.name: period_1947_1973
federal_reserve.description: Mean value for the period 1947:Q1 to 1973:Q1.

/equity/compare/groups.description:
Get company data grouped by sector, industry or country and display either performance or valuation metrics.

Valuation metrics include price to earnings, price to book, price to sales ratios and price to cash flow.
Performance metrics include the stock price change for different time periods.
/equity/compare/groups.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.compare.groups()
# Group by sector and analyze valuation.
obb.equity.compare.groups(group='sector', metric='valuation')
# Group by industry and analyze performance.
obb.equity.compare.groups(group='industry', metric='performance')
# Group by country and analyze valuation.
obb.equity.compare.groups(group='country', metric='valuation')
```

/equity/compare/company_facts.description: Compare reported company facts and fundamental data points.
/equity/compare/company_facts.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.compare.company_facts()
obb.equity.compare.company_facts(fact='PaymentsForRepurchaseOfCommonStock', year=2023)
obb.equity.compare.company_facts(symbol='NVDA,AAPL,AMZN,MSFT,GOOG,SMCI', fact='RevenueFromContractWithCustomerExcludingAssessedTax', year=2024)
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for. Multiple items allowed for provider(s): sec.

standard.name: fact
standard.description: The fact to lookup, typically a GAAP-reporting measure. Choices vary by provider.

sec.name: fact
sec.description: Fact or concept from the SEC taxonomy, in UpperCamelCase. Defaults to, 'Revenues'. AAPL, MSFT, GOOG, BRK-A currently report revenue as, 'RevenueFromContractWithCustomerExcludingAssessedTax'. In previous years, they have reported as 'Revenues'.

sec.name: year
sec.description: The year to retrieve the data for. If not provided, the current year is used. When symbol(s) are provided, excluding the year will return all reported values for the concept.

sec.name: fiscal_period
sec.description: The fiscal period to retrieve the data for. If not provided, the most recent quarter is used. This parameter is ignored when a symbol is supplied.

sec.name: instantaneous
sec.description:
Whether to retrieve instantaneous data. See the notes above for more information. Defaults to False. Some facts are only available as instantaneous data.
The function will automatically attempt the inverse of this parameter if the initial fiscal quarter request fails. This parameter is ignored when a symbol is supplied.

sec.name: use_cache
sec.description: Whether to use cache for the request. Defaults to True.

standard.name: period_ending
standard.description: The end date of the reporting period.

sec.description: The frame ID associated with the fact or concept, if applicable.

sec.description: The display name of the fact or concept.

/equity/discovery/filings.description:
Get the URLs to SEC filings reported to EDGAR database, such as 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, and more.

SEC filings include Form 10-K, Form 10-Q, Form 8-K, the proxy statement, Forms 3, 4, and 5, Schedule 13, Form 114,
Foreign Investment Disclosures and others. The annual 10-K report is required to be
filed annually and includes the company's financial statements, management discussion and analysis,
and audited financial statements.
/equity/discovery/filings.examples:

--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.discovery.filings()
```

/equity/fundamental/reported_financials.description: Get financial statements as reported by the company.
/equity/fundamental/reported_financials.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.fundamental.reported_financials(symbol='AAPL')
# Get AAPL balance sheet with a limit of 10 items.
obb.equity.fundamental.reported_financials(symbol='AAPL', period='annual', statement_type='balance', limit=10)
# Get reported income statement
obb.equity.fundamental.reported_financials(symbol='AAPL', statement_type='income')
# Get reported cash flow statement
obb.equity.fundamental.reported_financials(symbol='AAPL', statement_type='cash')
```

standard.name: period

standard.name: statement_type

standard.name: limit
standard.description: The number of data entries to return. Although the response object contains multiple results, because of the variance in the fields, year-to-year and quarter-to-quarter, it is recommended to view results in small chunks.

intrinio.name: period
intrinio.description: None

intrinio.name: statement_type
intrinio.description: Cash flow statements are reported as YTD, Q4 is the same as FY.

standard.description: The ending date of the reporting period.

/equity/fundamental/management_discussio.description: Get the Management Discussion & Analysis section from the financial statements for a given company.
/equity/fundamental/management_discussio.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.fundamental.management_discussion_analysis(symbol='AAPL')
# Get the Management Discussion & Analysis section by calendar year and period.
obb.equity.fundamental.management_discussion_analysis(symbol='AAPL', calendar_year=2020, calendar_period='Q4')
# Setting 'include_tables' to True will attempt to extract all tables in valid Markdown.
obb.equity.fundamental.management_discussion_analysis(symbol='AAPL', calendar_year=2020, calendar_period='Q4', include_tables=True)
# Setting 'raw_html' to True will bypass extraction and return the raw HTML file, as is. Use this for custom parsing or to access the entire HTML filing.
obb.equity.fundamental.management_discussion_analysis(symbol='AAPL', calendar_year=2020, calendar_period='Q4', raw_html=True)
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for.

standard.name: calendar_year
standard.description: Calendar year of the report. By default, is the current year. If the calendar period is not provided, but the calendar year is, it will return the annual report.

standard.name: calendar_period
standard.description: Calendar period of the report. By default, is the most recent report available for the symbol. If no calendar year and no calendar period are provided, it will return the most recent report.

sec.name: include_tables
sec.description: Return tables formatted as markdown in the text. Default is True.

sec.name: use_cache
sec.description: When True, the file will be cached for use later. Default is True.

sec.name: raw_html
sec.description: When True, the raw HTML content of the entire filing will be returned. Default is False. Use this option to parse the document manually.

/equity/ownership/form_13f.description:
Get the form 13F.

The Securities and Exchange Commission's (SEC) Form 13F is a quarterly report
that is required to be filed by all institutional investment managers with at least
$100 million in assets under management.
Managers are required to file Form 13F within 45 days after the last day of the calendar quarter.
Most funds wait until the end of this period in order to conceal
their investment strategy from competitors and the public.
/equity/ownership/form_13f.examples:
Examples
--------

```python
from openbb import obb
obb.equity.ownership.form_13f(symbol='NVDA')
# Enter a date (calendar quarter ending) for a specific report.
obb.equity.ownership.form_13f(symbol='BRK-A', date='2016-09-30')
# Example finding Michael Burry's filings.
cik = obb.regulators.sec.institutions_search("Scion Asset Management").results[0].cik
# Use the `limit` parameter to return N number of reports from the most recent.
obb.equity.ownership.form_13f(cik, limit=2).to_df()
```

standard.name: symbol
standard.description: Symbol to get data for. A CIK or Symbol can be used.

standard.name: date
standard.description: A specific date to get data for. The date represents the end of the reporting period. All form 13F-HR filings are based on the calendar year and are reported quarterly. If a date is not supplied, the most recent filing is returned. Submissions beginning 2013-06-30 are supported.

standard.name: limit
standard.description: The number of data entries to return. The number of previous filings to return. The date parameter takes priority over this parameter.

standard.name: period_ending
standard.description: The end-of-quarter date of the filing.

standard.name: issuer
standard.description: The name of the issuer.

standard.name: cusip
standard.description: The CUSIP of the security.

standard.name: asset_class
standard.description: The title of the asset class for the security.

standard.name: security_type
standard.description: Whether the principal amount represents the number of shares or the principal amount of such class. 'SH' for shares. 'PRN' for principal amount. Convertible debt securities are reported as 'PRN'.

standard.name: option_type
standard.description: Defined when the holdings being reported are put or call options. Only long positions are reported.

standard.name: investment_discretion
standard.description: The investment discretion held by the Manager. Sole, shared-defined (DFN), or shared-other (OTR).

standard.name: voting_authority_sole
standard.description: The number of shares for which the Manager exercises sole voting authority.

standard.name: voting_authority_shared
standard.description: The number of shares for which the Manager exercises a defined shared voting authority.

standard.name: voting_authority_none
standard.description: The number of shares for which the Manager exercises no voting authority.

standard.name: principal_amount
standard.description: The total number of shares of the class of security or the principal amount of such class. Defined by the 'security_type'. Only long positions are reported

standard.name: value
standard.description: The fair market value of the holding of the particular class of security. The value reported for options is the fair market value of the underlying security with respect to the number of shares controlled. Values are rounded to the nearest US dollar and use the closing price of the last trading day of the calendar year or quarter.

sec.name: weight
sec.description: The weight of the security relative to the market value of all securities in the filing , as a normalized percent.
